coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent structured color pigments based on CB@SiO2/CQDs core-shell nanospheres for solvent/UV dual-responsive anti-counterfeiting coating
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Bin
Cai, Jiajie
Hou, Maohua
Zhu, Meiting
Sheng, Xuanhao
Wang, Haolin
Pan, Li
Lu, Xiaohui
coredata.dc:description: Since traditional anti-counterfeiting technology with a single decryption mode is easy to be cracked and imitated, the development of new anti-counterfeiting technology with complex response modes has significance. Here, we propose a preparation method for solvent/ultraviolet (UV) dual-responsive anti-counterfeiting coating based on fluorescent structural color pigments. The fluorescent non-iridescent structural color pigments based on CB@SiO2/CQDs core-shell nanospheres were prepared by carbon black (CB) as the core, and SiO2 loaded with carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as the shell. The structural design of CB as the core can enhance the structural color saturation and enhance the chromatic stability. In addition, the CQDs embedded in SiO2 shells impart fluorescence function to structural color pigments, while effectively avoiding fluorescence quenching caused by agglomeration. Eventually, the solvent/UV dual-responsive photonic crystal anti-counterfeiting coatings were prepared by alternating spraying and superhydrophobic modification, consisting of structurally colored CB@SiO2, CB@SiO2/CQDs nanospheres and SiO2 nanospheres. The top SiO2 layer became transparent when the coatings were exposed to water. The underlying CB@SiO2 and CB@SiO2/CQDs composite coatings were impervious to water infiltration, owing to the superhydrophobic properties. The structural color and fluorescent pattern information can be observed under natural light and UV illumination, respectively, after the SiO2 layer became transparent. The solvent/UV dual-responsive anti-counterfeiting technology independent of large refractive index contrast was achieved by the above method, and this dual-responsive anti-counterfeiting strategy of fluorescence and structural color combination has a broad prospect in the field of anti-counterfeiting and information encryption.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CB@SiO2/CQDs
Fluorescence
Structural color
Solvent/UV dual-responsive
Anti-counterfeiting

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of silane-functionalized and non-silane-functionalized CQDs
Preparation of CB@SiO2 and SiO2 nanospheres
Preparation of solvent/UV dual-responsive anti-counterfeiting coatings
The phase structure and chemical composition of silane-functionalized CQDs
The structure and morphology of CB@SiO2/CQDs nanospheres
The optical properties of CB@SiO2/CQDs fluorescent structural color materials
Multiple anti-counterfeiting applications of CB@SiO2/CQDs fluorescent structured color materials

Zhejiang Wadou Creative Art Co. Ltd
Zhejiang Provincial Public Welfare Technology Application Projects of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by Zhejiang Provincial Public Welfare Technology Application Projects of China (Grant No. LGG22E090003) and Zhejiang Wadou Creative Art Co. Ltd (Grant No. KYY-HX-20230400).

This research was supported by Zhejiang Provincial Public Welfare Technology Application Projects of China (Grant No. LGG22E090003) and Zhejiang Wadou Creative Art Co., Ltd (Grant No. KYY-HX-20230400).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a–d) TEM images of (a, b) silane-functionalized CQDs, (c) CB particles, (d) CB@SiO2/CQDs nanosphere (bright field). (e) TEM image (dark field) and element mapping of the CB@SiO2/CQDs nanosphere.

(a) Optical microscopy images of structurally colored films and powders prepared from CB@SiO2/CQDs nanospheres with different particle sizes. (b) SEM and particle size distribution images of the three CB@SiO2/CQDs nanospheres. (c) Photographs of dry powder blocks assembled from CB@SiO2/CQDs nanospheres. (d) Reflectance spectra and (e) CIE chromaticity coordinates of the samples in (a). Scale bar: 500 μm.

(a) Photographs of structurally colored coatings prepared from CB@SiO2/CQDs nanospheres with different particle sizes at different viewing angles. (b) Photographs of structurally colored coatings under UV illumination. (c) Photographs of colored patterns spray-coated from CB@SiO2/CQDs nanospheres with different particle sizes under day light and UV illumination. (d) Photographs of colored patterns in d at different viewing angles. (e) SEM image of the coating. The inset shows the 2D fourier transform of the SEM image. Scale bars: (a, b) 1 cm, (c, d) 2 cm.
Preparation and decryption process of solvent/UV dual-responsive anti-counterfeiting coating.
Photographs of solvent/UV dual-responsive anti-counterfeiting coating during (a) preparation and (b) decryption process.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent structured color pigments based on CB@SiO2/CQDs core-shell nanospheres for solvent/UV dual-responsive anti-counterfeiting coating

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Zhejiang University of Technology, Hangzhou, 310014, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Zhejiang University of Technology, Hangzhou, 310014, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Mechanical Engineering, Zhejiang University of Technology, Hangzhou, 310023, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Mechanical Engineering, Zhejiang University of Technology, Hangzhou, 310023, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Mechanical and Energy Engineering, Zhejiang University of Science and Technology, Hangzhou, 310023, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Mechanical and Energy Engineering
Zhejiang University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Mechanical and Energy Engineering, Zhejiang University of Science and Technology, Hangzhou, 310023, China;

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Since traditional anti-counterfeiting technology with a single decryption mode is easy to be cracked and imitated, the development of new anti-counterfeiting technology with complex response modes has significance. Here, we propose a preparation method for solvent/ultraviolet (UV) dual-responsive anti-counterfeiting coating based on fluorescent structural color pigments. The fluorescent non-iridescent structural color pigments based on CB@SiO2/CQDs core-shell nanospheres were prepared by carbon black (CB) as the core, and SiO2 loaded with carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as the shell. The structural design of CB as the core can enhance the structural color saturation and enhance the chromatic stability. In addition, the CQDs embedded in SiO2 shells impart fluorescence function to structural color pigments, while effectively avoiding fluorescence quenching caused by agglomeration. Eventually, the solvent/UV dual-responsive photonic crystal anti-counterfeiting coatings were prepared by alternating spraying and superhydrophobic modification, consisting of structurally colored CB@SiO2, CB@SiO2/CQDs nanospheres and SiO2 nanospheres. The top SiO2 layer became transparent when the coatings were exposed to water. The underlying CB@SiO2 and CB@SiO2/CQDs composite coatings were impervious to water infiltration, owing to the superhydrophobic properties. The structural color and fluorescent pattern information can be observed under natural light and UV illumination, respectively, after the SiO2 layer became transparent. The solvent/UV dual-responsive anti-counterfeiting technology independent of large refractive index contrast was achieved by the above method, and this dual-responsive anti-counterfeiting strategy of fluorescence and structural color combination has a broad prospect in the field of anti-counterfeiting and information encryption.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the development of science and technology, new types of counterfeiting techniques are emerging, and the problem of counterfeiting is becoming increasingly serious, which greatly damages the reputation of enterprises and the interests of consumers [1,2]. Against this background, anti-counterfeiting has become an enduring research hotspot, widely intertwined with people's daily life, such as banknotes, commodity packaging, ID cards and copyrighted materials. However, traditional anti-counterfeiting technologies, such as barcode [3,4], watermarking [5,6] and laser holograms [7] are susceptible to cracking and imitation by lawless elements due to their fixed images and colors, simple and single decryption modes. Therefore, the development of novel anti-counterfeiting technologies with intricate response modes holds immense significance.

Responsive photonic crystal structure color materials have attracted extensive attention in the field of information encryption and optical anti-counterfeiting, due to the variable color and diverse response types [8,9]. Photonic crystals are ordered structure materials formed by two or more media with different permittivity arranged periodically in space [10,11]. Physical structure color produced by the interaction between photonic crystal periodic structure and visible light has the advantages of stable chemical properties, no pollution and no fading [12–14]. Based on the unique optical properties of photonic crystals, researchers have developed a variety of responsive structural color materials, such as solvent response [15–17], magnetic field response [18,19], electric field response [20], thermal response [21–23] and light response [24,25]. Wen et al. [26] achieved solvent/UV-driven information encryption based on multilayer quasi-amorphous photonic structures by alternately spraying SiO2, Fe3O4@SiO2, and CdSe/ZnS quantum dots, taking advantage of the high refractive index of Fe3O4@SiO2 and the fluorescence properties of CdSe/ZnS quantum dots. However, this method is not applicable to photonic crystal structures with low refractive index contrast, and CdSe/ZnS quantum dots deposited directly on the photonic crystal layer are prone to agglomerate and lead to fluorescence quenching. In addition, the heavy metal element Cd is inconsistent with the green concept. Yu et al. [25] functionalized silane of eco-friendly CQDs prepare SiO2 colloidal spheres concentrically embedded in carbon dots (SiO2/CD). The photonic pigment based on SiO2/CD colloidal spheres has a stable UV excitation fluorescence function, and the saturation of the pigment can be increased by doping carbon black, so that it is not limited by the backside. The photonic pigment, based on SiO2/CD colloidal spheres, exhibits a stable UV excitation fluorescence function. Furthermore, the saturation of the pigment can be enhanced through carbon black doping, thereby eliminating limitations imposed by the backside. However, this strategy only offers a single mode of response, leading to a low level of anti-counterfeiting measures. Additionally, carbon black is prone to reunite because of its high surface energy, resulting in poor color stability of the pigment.

In this study, using CB as core and SiO2 loaded with CQDs as shell, fluorescent non-iridescent structural color pigments based on CB@SiO2/CQDs core-shell nanospheres were prepared. CB was utilized to absorb incoherently scattered light and improved the structural color saturation. The core-shell structure design avoided the reunion of CB with time and improved the color stability of structural color pigments. The inexpensive and eco-friendly CQDs were silanized and embedded in SiO2, which can avoid the fluorescence quenching triggered by aggregation. Subsequently, the solvent/UV dual-responsive anti-counterfeiting strategy was proposed by combining the superhydrophobic properties with the optical properties of structural color materials. The coating was prepared by alternating spraying and fluorination, consisting of CB@SiO2, CB@SiO2/CQDs nanospheres with the same structural color and white pure SiO2 nanospheres. The coating can be decrypted through water droplet impregnation and UV irradiation without relying on high refractive index contrast. The fluorescent non-iridescent structural color pigment and the anti-counterfeiting application strategy have potential application value in the fields of sensing, multi-information encryption and optical anti-counterfeiting.

Carbon black MA-100 was supplied from Mitsubishi Chemical Corporation. Anhydrous ethanol (AR) was purchased from Hangzhou Shuangmu Chemical Co., Ltd. Tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS, 98 %), citric acid (AR), N-(β-aminoethyl-γ-aminopropyl) methyldimethoxysilane (AEAPMS, 96 %), trimethoxy (1H,1H,2H,2H-heptadecafluorodecyl) silane (TMHFDS, 98 %) and urea (AR) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Ammonium hydroxide (NH3•H2O, 25–28 %) from Shanghai Maclin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Dialysis bag (MD44-500) was obtained from JIELEPU, USA. The deionized water is homemade.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of silane-functionalized and non-silane-functionalized CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The silane functionalized CQDs were prepared by one-pot hydrothermal method [27]. The citric acid (0.25 g) was dissolved in deionized water (5 mL), and AEAPMS (5 mL) was mixed. Then the mixed solution was transferred to a high-pressure hydrothermal reactor with a capacity of 25 mL, and the reactor was placed in an oven and heated at 180 °C for 12 h. After the reaction, the product was cooled to room temperature and dialyzed in deionized water for 24 h. The molecular weight of the dialysis bag was 500 Da. The preparation process of non-silane functionalized carbon point is as follows: citric acid (0.25 g) and urea (0.25 g) were dissolved in deionized water (10 mL), and the mixed solution is transferred to the hydrothermal reactor. The other steps were same as the above method.

The CB@SiO2/CQDs nanospheres with different sizes were synthesized by altering the TEOS concentration using the modified Stӧber method. Specifically, the 263 nm of CB@SiO2/CQDs nanospheres were synthesized by ultrasonically dispersing carbon black particles (40 mg) into anhydrous ethanol (32 mL) for 1 h, and then put the carbon black suspension into a 250 mL three-port flask. Subsequently, 8 mL of ammonium hydroxide and 3 mL of deionized water were added into the three-port flask, which were heated at 60 °C and mechanical stirred with 400 rpm for 30 min. Then, 3 mL of TEOS, 300 μL of silane-functionalized CQDs mother liquid and 15 mL of anhydrous ethanol mixture were added into the three-port flask and continued to stir for 2 h. After the reaction, the product was centrifuged and washed with anhydrous ethanol and deionized water for three times, and dried in an oven at 50 °C for 12 h. Eventually, the CB@SiO2/CQDs nanosphere powders were obtained after grinding.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CB@SiO2 and SiO2 nanospheres
ce:section.ce:para: The CB@SiO2 nanospheres were prepared without the addition of CQDs mother liquid, and the SiO2 nanospheres were prepared without the addition of CQDs mother liquid and carbon black particles, which the other steps were consistent with the preparation method of CB@SiO2/CQDs nanospheres.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of solvent/UV dual-responsive anti-counterfeiting coatings
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesized SiO2, CB@SiO2 and CB@SiO2/CQDs nanospheres were dispersed in ethanol (5 wt%), respectively, and ultrasonicated for 1 h. The coatings were prepared by a spray gun with a nozzle size of 0.3 mm, the distance between the gun and substrate was 5 cm. Initially, the CB@SiO2 nanospheres were sprayed on the stainless steel plate matrix of 50 × 50 × 1 mm by a mask to prepare non-fluorescent structural color patterns. Then, the CB@SiO2/CQDs nanospheres were sprayed by masks of other shapes to prepare fluorescence structural color patterns. Both coatings were used the same structural color. On this basis, the surface of the structural color coating was modified by chemical vapor deposition. The TMHFDS was mixed with deionized water (0.2 wt%), and then 20 mL of the mixture and the structural color coating were placed in a closed container with a capacity of 300 mL. The hydrophobic modified structural color coating was obtained after the mixture was completely dried in the oven at 125 °C. Finally, the SiO2 nanospheres were sprayed to cover the structural color to obtain the solvent/UV dual-responsive anti-counterfeiting coating.

The surface morphologies of the photonic crystals were observed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, ZEISS SIGMA) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Tecnai G2 F30 STWIN). One hundred particles in SEM images were randomly selected for particle size statistics. The internal lattice features and the physical structure of the constituents of samples were analyzed by X-ray diffractometer (XRD, Panalytical X'PERT PRO) with 2θ range of 10–80° and a scanning speed of 10°/min (40 kV, 20 mA). The chemical components of silane-functionalized CQDs were analyzed by Fourier infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, Thermo Scientific Nicolet 6700). The fluorescence spectra of silane-functionalized CQDs were obtained by a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi High-Technologies F-4600). The reflectance spectra of CB@SiO2/CQDs structural color coatings were obtained via an ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) spectrophotometer (Cary 5000 UV–Vis–NIR). The contact angle of the structural color coating was measured by an optical contact angle measuring instrument (Dataphysics OCA30).

The preparation mechanism of CB@SiO2/CQDs nanospheres was depicted in Scheme 1 . Firstly, the silane-functionalized CQDs were prepared by hydrothermal method, using citric acid as carbon source and AEAPMS as passivating agent. The citric acid formed carbon nuclei after dehydration, polymerization and carbonization processes. The amine functional group of AEAPMS amidated with the carboxyl group on the surface of the carbon nucleus, resulting in the growth of the nucleus into silane-functionalized CQDs. Then, the CQDs hybrid silica coated CB particles were prepared in one step by using the modified Stӧber method. The shell thickness could be adjusted by changing the concentration of TEOS. As the surface of CB particles was rich in hydroxyl groups, under alkaline conditions, the TEOS hydrolyzed on the surface of CB and condensed to form Si–O–Si bond. Meanwhile, CQDs with silane on the surface also participated in the reaction process, so that CQDs were doped in the three-dimensional skeleton structure of long chain in silicon oxide to form CB@SiO2/CQDs nanospheres.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The phase structure and chemical composition of silane-functionalized CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The XRD pattern of silanized CQDs (Fig. S1) shows a wide peak centered at 2θ of 21°, which was consistent with the (0 0 2) lattice spacing of carbon-based materials with turbine disorder. The FT-IR spectrum of silane-functionalized CQDs was shown in Fig. S2. The absorption peaks at 1568 cm−1 and 3290 cm−1 correspond to the N–H bond tensile vibration and bending vibration peaks in the amide bond, respectively, and the absorption peak at 1668 cm−1 was derived from the C=O bond tensile vibration in the amide bond. This result proved the existence of amide bonds. The absorption peaks at 920 cm−1 and 1010 cm−1 correspond to the characteristic peaks of Si–OH and Si–O–C, respectively. During the formation of the silica shell, under alkaline conditions, the Si–OH group on the surface of the CQDs and the Si–OH in the Si–O network of the silicon oxide underwent dehydration condensation, so that the CQDs were hybridized in the silicon oxide shell. In addition, the Si–O–CH3 group can be hydrolyzed to Si–OH, thus participating in the above reaction process. The results show AEAPMS successfully amideates with the carboxyl group on CQDs surface and modify silane on CQDs surface.

The fluorescence spectrum of silanized CQDs solution was shown in Fig. S3a, with the fluorescence excitation peak center at 375 nm and emission peak center at 445 nm. The photographs of the silanized CQDs solution under natural and ultraviolet illumination were shown in the inset. The solution was nearly transparent under natural light, while fluoresced bright blue under ultraviolet illumination. Fig. S3b showed the emission spectra of silanized CQDs solution at different excitation wavelengths. When the excitation wavelength changed between 330 nm and 390 nm, the intensity of the emission peak changed, but the position of the emission peak did not change. The above results demonstrate the fluorescence color of silanized CQDs is independent of the excitation light. The emission peak center is 445 nm, and the emission light is the strongest at the excitation wavelength of 375 nm, which is consistent with the excitation wavelength in Fig. S3a.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The structure and morphology of CB@SiO2/CQDs nanospheres
ce:section.ce:para: The TEM images of silane-functionalized CQDs were shown in Fig. 1 a and b. The size range of CQDs is about 2–5 nm, and the lattice spacing is 0.332 nm, corresponding to the (0 0 2) crystal face of graphite. The TEM image of CB particles was shown in Fig. 1c. The particle size of CB particles is about 20–30 nm, which agglomerated due to the high surface energy. The TEM image (bright field) of CB@SiO2/CQDs nanosphere was shown in Fig. 1d, where SiO2 was successfully encapsulated on the surface of CB particles to form a uniform silica shell layer. However, due to the large thickness of the silicon shell and the small number of carbon atoms, the core-shell structure shown in the TEM image is not clear. Fig. 1e showed the TEM imagen (dark field) of CB@SiO2/CQDs nanosphere and the spatial distribution of C, Si, and O elements. The monodisperse CB particles with a size of about 25 nm are located in the center of the nanosphere, instead of showing the agglomerated state as shown in Fig. 1c. For C element, in addition to the strong C signal from CB particles in the central region, other regions also show a uniform distribution of C element, which comes from the uniform distribution of CQDs in the SiO2 shell. The results show the silane-functionalized CQDs are successfully hybridized in SiO2 shells, forming CB@SiO2/CQDs core-shell nanospheres.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The optical properties of CB@SiO2/CQDs fluorescent structural color materials
ce:section.ce:para:
The CB@SiO2/CQDs nanospheres with particle sizes of 261, 298 and 367 nm were synthesized by regulating the concentration of TEOS (Fig. 2b), and the corresponding amounts of TEOS were 3, 4 and 5 mL, respectively. The optical microscopic images of the structural color films and powders of the three samples were shown in Fig. 2a. As photonic crystal structure units, the CB@SiO2/CQDs nanospheres of different sizes show different structural colors. In particular, it can be observed the structural color powder after grinding still has a high color saturation. This result is due to the existence of small volume photonic structure in the structural color powder, and this tiny photonic crystal can still interact with light to produce structural color (Figure S4). Therefore, the CB@SiO2/CQDs structural color powder can be used directly as a pigment. To demonstrate the color stability of CB@SiO2/CQDs structural color pigment, it was re-dispersed in anhydrous ethanol, ultrasonic for 1 h, and dried at 60 °C. The structural color dry powder assembled by the CB@SiO2/CQDs nanospheres was obtained by evaporation-induced self-assembly. As shown in Fig. 2c, uneven color due to CB agglomeration was not observed. Compared to physically doped carbon black particles, the structural design of CB as a nucleus enables structural color pigments to have stronger color stability, which has been demonstrated in our previous work [28]. Fig. 2d shows the corresponding reflection spectra of the three samples. The CB@SiO2/CQDs with particle sizes of 261 nm and 298 nm both have a strong reflection in the low wavelength region except for the reflection peaks at 538 nm and 639 nm, respectively. For the 367 nm size of CB@SiO2/CQDs structural color coating, in addition to the reflection peak at 456 nm, there is also a strong reflection in the long wavelength range. This result means the colors of CB@SiO2/CQDs structural color coating are compound and there are double reflection peaks, which Aguirre et al. [29] also found. The reflection in the low wavelength region comes from Mie resonance backscattering of individual particles [30,31]. As the size of CB@SiO2/CQDs nanospheres increases, the reflection peak redshifts, which can be explained by the following formula [32]:
(1)
D λ
∝
1
n eff

D is the distance between two adjacent spheres, λ is the wavelength of the reflection peak, and
n eff
is the effective refractive index. The refractive index of core-shell nanospheres can be expressed by the following formula:
(2)
n particle
=
n
SiO 2
/ CQDs
[
1 −
(
r / R
)
3
]
+
n CB
(
r / R
)
3

The
n eff
is the refractive index of the CB@SiO2/CQDs nanosphere, where r and R indicate the radius of CB and CB@SiO2/CQDs, respectively. The results show the central position of the reflection peak is positively correlated with the effective refractive index, which increases with the increase of CB@SiO2/CQDs nanosphere size, resulting in the redshift of both reflection peaks. In order to quantitatively describe the color saturation of the samples, accurate chromaticity coordinates were obtained based on the reflection spectra of each sample and plotted on the 1931 chromaticity diagram of the International Commission on Illumination (CIE), as shown in Fig. 2 e. The CIE coordinates of 261, 298 and 367 nm CB@SiO2/CQDs structural color coatings were (0.2582, 0.3087), (0.3305, 0.3864) and (0.3353, 0.2542), respectively.

Fig. 3 a shows photographs of 261, 298, and 367 nm CB@SiO2/CQD fluorescent structural color coating under naturally scattered light with different viewing angles. The viewing angle θ was the angle between the normal direction of the coating and the viewing direction. The structural color coating presents the same color at 30°, 60° and 90° viewing angles, which means the structural color of the coating has angle-independent properties. The three samples all showed blue fluorescence under ultraviolet illumination, with uniform fluorescence and high intensity (Fig. 3b). The fluorescent color patterns can be prepared over a large area by spraying CB@SiO2/CQDs nanospheres with different particle sizes on the substrate with a mask. Fig. 3c, d shows the fluorescent structure color pot pattern prepared on a white polypropylene plastic sheet. Under natural light, the CB can absorb non-coherent scattered light, enabling the CB@SiO2/CQDs structural color coating to exhibit highly saturated non-iridescent structural colors even on white backing, independent of the backing and viewing angle (Fig. 3d). Under ultraviolet illumination, the structural color was hidden, and the pattern presented a bright blue fluorescence (Fig. 3c). The fourier component ring characteristics indicate the fluorescent structural color coating has a short-range ordered structure (Fig. 3e). This result is due to the rapid solvent volatilization rate during the spraying process, and the nanoparticles do not have enough time to complete self-assembly, contributing to this isotropic amorphous structure.

In order to confirm the CQDs were encapsulated in SiO2 shell rather than free in solvent, centrifugal experiments were designed. A mixed solution (CB@SiO2-CQDs) was prepared by dispersing non-fluorescent CB@SiO2 nanospheres with non-silane-functionalized CQDs in deionized water. The phenomena of CB@SiO2 nanosphere suspension, CB@SiO2-CQDs mixed solution, and CB@SiO2/CQDs nanosphere suspension before and after centrifugation under different illumination conditions were compared with those of carbon-free particle points (Fig. S5). Before centrifugation, none of the three solutions fluoresced under natural light. Under ultraviolet illumination, the solutions of CB@SiO2-CQDs and CB@SiO2/CQDs presented strong bright blue fluorescence. After centrifugation, the nanospheres in the three solutions were centrifuged to the bottom of the solution. Under ultraviolet illumination, both the supernatant and bottom precipitate of the CB@SiO2 suspension were non-fluorescent, the bottom precipitate of the CB@SiO2-CQDs mixture solution was non-fluorescent, and the supernatant showed strong fluorescence. In contrast, the CB@SiO2/CQDs nanosphere suspension, the bottom precipitation showed uniform bright blue fluorescence under ultraviolet illumination, while the supernatant showed no fluorescence. The above results reveal the silanized CQDs were successfully hybridized in the silica shell, and the CB@SiO2/CQDs nanospheres still exhibit uniform and strong fluorescence even after centrifugation.

Fig. S6 shows photographs of structural color coatings and powders prepared from the CB@SiO2, CB@SiO2-CQDs, and CB@SiO2/CQDs. The three structural color coatings all exhibited bright structural colors under natural light. Under ultraviolet illumination, the CB@SiO2 structural color coating without CQDs had no fluoresced as expected, while the CB@SiO2-CQDs and CB@SiO2/CQDs structural color coatings all exhibited blue fluorescence. However, the fluorescence intensity produced by CB@SiO2-CQDs was low, and the fluorescence distribution was not uniform, which was due to the fluorescence quenching phenomenon caused by the aggregation of CQDs in the solid state. For the structural color coating prepared by CB@SiO2/CQDs nanospheres, strong blue fluorescence was still presented even in the solid state, and the fluorescence distribution was uniform. This result was due to the silicon dioxide as a carrier can rivet the CQDs in its interior by the dehydration condensation between silicon hydroxyl groups. The uniform rivet of CQDs effectively avoided the phenomenon of fluorescence quenching caused by excessive local concentration of CQDs. Moreover, compare to the coating state, the three samples in powder state also showed bright structural colors. Under ultraviolet illumination, the fluorescence of CB@SiO2-CQDs structural color powders was not observed at all due to the higher degree of carbon aggregation in powder accumulation state, while the CB@SiO2/CQDs structural color powders still had high fluorescence intensity. To investigate the fluorescence stability of CB@SiO2/CQDs, the samples of CB@SiO2/CQDs were illuminated by 10 W of UV light at 365 nm in an air atmosphere at room temperature. The temporal evolution of the relative PL intensity of the samples is shown in Fig. S7. Within 1 h of illumination, the PL intensity of CB@SiO2/CQDs decreased to approximately 24 % of the initial value. The results suggest that the CB@SiO2/CQDs structured color pigments have high fluorescence intensity, good fluorescence stability and uniform fluorescence distribution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Multiple anti-counterfeiting applications of CB@SiO2/CQDs fluorescent structured color materials
ce:section.ce:para: The above advantages of CB@SiO2/CQDs fluorescent structural color pigments provide strong support for its application in the field of anti-counterfeiting. A solvent/UV dual-responsive anti-counterfeiting coating was prepared based on CB@SiO2/CQDs fluorescent structural color pigment. Fig. 4 shows the preparation and decryption process of the solvent/UV dual-responsive anti-counterfeiting coating. A non-fluorescent non-iridescence structural color pattern was prepared by spraying CB@SiO2 nanospheres on the substrate with a mask. Then, CB@SiO2/CQDs fluorescent structural color pigments, which had the same structural color as CB@SiO2 coating, were sprayed on the structural color coating prepared in the previous step by using masks of other shapes to prepare fluorescent structural color patterns, and CB@SiO2-CB@SiO2/CQDs bilayer coatings were formed. Due to the same structural color of the two coatings, only the CB@SiO2 structural color pattern can be observed under natural light, and the CB@SiO2/CQDs fluorescence pattern can be observed under ultraviolet illumination. Further, the TMHFDS was used to modify the surface of the coating by vapor deposition. The surface of the coating was fluorinated through dealcoholization polycondensation between silane and hydroxyl group on the coating surface to reduce its surface energy. Due to the combination of micro-nano structure and low surface energy, the structural color coating has super hydrophobic properties. Finally, SiO2 nanospheres without CB were sprayed on the surface of the coating to form a SiO2 photonic crystal layer, and the CB@SiO2-CB@SiO2/CQDs bilayer coatings were covered to prepare a solvent/UV dual-responsive anti-counterfeiting coating.

Under the effect of strongly incoherent scattered light, the SiO2 photonic crystal layer appeared as pure white, effectively hiding the fluorescence and structural color patterns. As a result, the coating appeared white in both daylight and ultraviolet illumination. When the air in the topmost SiO2 photonic crystal array was completely filled with water, the refractive index contrast between silica and the medium was greatly reduced because the refractive index of water (
n water
= 1.33
) was similar to that of silica (
n
SiO 2
= 1.45), making the pure SiO2 coating nearly transparent. However, the CB@SiO2-CB@SiO2/CQDs bilayer coatings under the SiO2 coating would not be infiltrated by water due to its superhydrophobic properties, and the refractive index was unchanged. Therefore, the structural color was not affected, and the CB@SiO2 structural color pattern information was revealed, and the first decryption was completed. On this basis, ultraviolet illumination of specific wavelength was irradiated through the transparent SiO2 layer, and CB@SiO2/CQDs on the CB@SiO2 coating was excited, showing fluorescence pattern information. At this time, the second decryption was completed.

The preparation scheme of the solvent/UV dual-responsive anti-counterfeiting coating mentioned above involves superhydrophobic modification of the fluorescence structural color coating. Considering fluorination treatment might affect the structural color and fluorescence properties, the optical properties of CB@SiO2-CB@SiO2/CQDs bilayer coatings and CB@SiO2/CQDs fluorescent structural color coating before and after superhydrophobic modification were compared. Figure S8 (a) shows the photographs before and after the hydrophobic modification of the structural color pattern of "bird on a branch" prepared by spraying CB@SiO2 and CB@SiO2/CQDs nanospheres successively on the handmade paper matrix by different masks. Before hydrophobic modification, CB@SiO2/CQDs fluorescence structural color pattern was hidden under natural light, and only blue "bird on a branch " structural color pattern could be observed. Under UV illumination, the carbon dots in the CB@SiO2/CQDs nanospheres were stimulated, and the structural color coating showed fluorescent patterns in the shapes of the sun, the moon, and the airplane. After hydrophobic modification, the structure color of the coating did not change, and still had a high saturation structure color under natural light, as well as presented a bright blue fluorescence pattern under ultraviolet illumination. Figure S8 (b) shows the photographs and reflection spectra of the fluorescent structural color coating prepared by 261, 298 and 367 nm CB@SiO2/CQDs nanospheres before and after hydrophobic modification. The color of the fluorescent structural color coating before and after hydrophobic modification was similar, as well as the position and intensity of the corresponding reflection peak did no change. The results demonstrate the hydrophobic modification has no effect on the structure color and fluorescence properties of the coatings.

To assess the fluorescence and color stability of the hydrophobically modified coatings in an aqueous environment, the coatings were immersed in water for 1 h. As shown in Fig. S9, the fluorescence intensity decreased to approximately 25 % of its original value, and the reflectivity of the coating also declined. This degradation is primarily attributed to the penetration of water into the microscopic gaps between the hydrophobically modified CB@SiO2/CQDs nanospheres and the glass substrate. Capillary forces facilitated the infiltration of water, weakening the adhesion between the coating and the substrate, which led to powder detachment and a subsequent reduction in both fluorescence intensity and reflectivity [33].

Fig. 5 a shows a photograph of solvent/UV dual-responsive anti-counterfeiting coating during the preparation processes. A non-fluorescent structure color pattern in the shape of a butterfly was prepared by spraying CB@SiO2 nanospheres on a stainless steel plate with a mask. Then, CB@SiO2/CQDs nanospheres with the same structural color as CB@SiO2 were sprayed on CB@SiO2 coating by other patterned masks to obtain CB@SiO2-CB@SiO2/CQDs bilayer coatings. Under natural light, the structure color coating of CB@SiO2/CQDs nanospheres showed no color change before and after spraying, and the fluorescence pattern information of CB@SiO2/CQDs was effectively hidden. Under ultraviolet illumination, CB@SiO2/CQDs fluorescence nanospheres were excited, and the fluorescence pattern information was obtained. The fluorescence pattern of number "0 1 2″ was displayed on the butterfly-shaped structural color pattern. Further, the coating was fluorinated with TMHFDS to reduce the surface energy of the fluorescence structure color coating. The 4 μL of deionized water were added to the hydrophobic modified structural color coating. At this time, the water droplets did not infiltrate the coating, while formed a ball on the surface of the coating. The measured contact angle was 153.2°, indicating the coating had reached superhydrophobic status. Finally, the pure SiO2 nanospheres were sprayed onto the whole substrate, and the fluorescent structural color dual anti-counterfeiting coatings were prepared. The CB@SiO2-CB@SiO2/CQDs bilayer coatings were completely covered by SiO2 layer, and both structural color and fluorescence pattern information were hidden.

The decryption process is shown in Fig. 5b. When the coating was moistened by water, the refractive index contrast of the upper SiO2 photonic crystal layer was greatly reduced, making it tend to be transparent. While the CB@SiO2-CB@SiO2/CQDs bilayer coatings underneath were not infiltrated by superhydrophobicity, the refractive index contrast was unchanged, and the blue butterfly-shaped structural color pattern could be clearly observed, and the first information was acquired. Additionally, the ultraviolet illumination with wavelength of 375 nm was used to irradiate the water-impregnated surface of the coating, and a bright blue “0 1 2” fluorescent pattern was shown on the blue butterfly-shaped structural color pattern, thus the decryption was completed, and the dual information of fluorescent structural color was obtained. The solvent/UV dual-response anti-counterfeiting technology independent of high refractive index contrast was realized.

In summary, the fluorescent non-iridescence structural color pigments based on CB@SiO2/CQDs core-shell nanospheres have been successfully prepared. The pigments had UV-responsive fluorescence function and were unrestricted by black backside. This core-shell structure design not only solved the problem of CB reunion with time, but also effectively avoided fluorescence quenching caused by CQDs agglomeration, which greatly improved the color stability and fluorescence stability of pigments. The desired pigment color could be obtained by regulating the amount of TEOS. Furthermore, a solvent/UV dual-responsive anti-counterfeiting coating based on CB@SiO2, CB@SiO2/CQDs and SiO2 nanospheres was designed by using multi-layer spraying and fluorination. This solvent/UV dual-responsive anti-counterfeiting coating provided fast access to dual information of structural color and fluorescent pattern information by water drop impregnation and UV illumination, and did not rely on high refractive index contrast. This combination of fluorescent structured color pigments and multiple response information encryption strategy has broad application prospects in the fields of sensing, information encryption and anti-counterfeiting.

Bin Xu: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Methodology, Conceptualization. Jiajie Cai: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Investigation, Data curation. Maohua Hou: Visualization, Investigation, Data curation. Meiting Zhu: Resources, Investigation. Xuanhao Sheng: Resources. Haolin Wang: Resources. Li Pan: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Xiaohui Lu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: We declare that we do not have any commercial or associative interest that represents a conflict of interest in connection with the work submitted.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by Zhejiang Provincial Public Welfare Technology Application Projects of China (Grant No. LGG22E090003) and Zhejiang Wadou Creative Art Co., Ltd (Grant No. KYY-HX-20230400).
